perturbation.original_text: (iii) CELEBRITY will receive $6,000 per month for entire duration of the agreement. The COMPANY will give stock to the CELEBRITY which will be restricted for one year from issuance and will be as follows: Monthly Sales Volume Stock Options -------------------- ------------- $ 300,000 300,000 shares 600,000 600,000 shares 1,000,000 1,000,000 shares These stock options will remain in place until certificates are issued once volumes are met as long as the CELEBRITY maintains the distributorship by paying the annual dues of $15.00.
perturbation.changed_text: (iii) CELEBRITY will receive $6,000 per month for entire duration of the agreement. The COMPANY will give stock to the CELEBRITY which will be restricted for ten years from issuance and will be as follows: Monthly Sales Volume Stock Options -------------------- ------------- $ 300,000 300,000 shares 600,000 600,000 shares 1,000,000 1,000,000 shares These stock options will remain in place until certificates are issued once volumes are met as long as the CELEBRITY maintains the distributorship by paying the annual dues of $15.00.
perturbation.explanation: The modification extends the restriction period for the stock options to ten years, which could potentially conflict with securities regulations if it's considered an unreasonable restraint on alienation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 144 under the Securities Act of 1933 sets conditions for the resale of restricted securities. Extending the restriction period to ten years might make it difficult for the CELEBRITY to sell the shares within a reasonable timeframe compliant with Rule 144, potentially rendering the stock options less valuable and creating a regulatory issue if the restrictions are deemed excessively long or unreasonable.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.144 - Persons deemed not to be engaged in a distribution and therefore not underwriters. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.144 - Persons deemed not to be engaged in a distribution and therefore not underwriters. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.144 Persons deemed not to be engaged in a distribution and therefore not underwriters. Preliminary Note: Certain basic principles are essential to an understanding of the registration requirements in the Securities Act of 1933 (the Act or the Securities Act) and the purposes underlying Rule 144: 1. If any person sells a non-exempt security to any other person, the sale must be registered unless an exemption can be found for the transaction. 2. Section 4(1) of the Securities Act provides one such exemption for a transaction “by a person other than an issuer , underwriter , or dealer.” Therefore, an understanding of the term “underwriter” is important in determining whether or not the Section 4(1) exemption from registration is available for the sale of the securities. The term “underwriter” is broadly defined in Section 2(a)(11) of the Securities Act to mean any person who has purchased from an issuer with a view to, or offers or sells for an issuer in connection with, the distribution of any security , or participates , or has a direct or indirect participation in any such undertaking, or participates or has a participation in the direct or indirect underwriting of any such undertaking. The interpretation of this definition traditionally has focused on the words “with a view to” in the phrase “purchased from an issuer with a view to * * * distribution .” An investment banking firm which arranges with an issuer for the public sale of its securities is clearly an “underwriter” under that section. However, individual investors who are not professionals in the securities business also may be “underwrit
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the restriction period for stock options to ten years, and this longer restriction could create an issue with the resale of restricted securities under Rule 144. The Law Explanation suggests this might be an unreasonable restraint, potentially conflicting with securities regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Rule 144, states that if a person sells a non-exempt security, the sale must be registered unless an exemption can be found. The extended restriction period could make it more difficult to find an exemption for resale, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (iv) The terms of this agreement will remain confidential between CELEBRITY and the COMPANY unless written permission is granted for release by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: (iv) The terms of this agreement will remain confidential between CELEBRITY and the COMPANY even if required to be disclosed by law or legal proceedings.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text now attempts to supersede legal requirements for disclosure, which is generally unenforceable and could result in legal penalties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26
perturbation.law_explanation: NRCP 26 governs discovery in Nevada civil proceedings, mandating the disclosure of relevant information, regardless of contractual confidentiality clauses. Modifying the contract to prevent disclosure even when legally required directly contradicts the Nevada Rules of Civil Procedure regarding discovery and compliance with court orders.

perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to override legal requirements for disclosure, creating a direct conflict with legal obligations. The original text ensures confidentiality 'unless written permission is granted', implying adherence to legal requirements when permissions are not granted. The modified text, however, states confidentiality applies 'even if required to be disclosed by law', which clearly contradicts potential legal mandates for disclosure. While the provided snippets do not explicitly outline laws on disclosure, the LLM explanation referencing NRCP 26 suggests legal proceedings may mandate the disclosure of confidential information. By attempting to supersede such legal requirements, the modified contract introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Travel arrangements and accommodations will be provided by the COMPANY. This will be first class accommodations when available.
perturbation.changed_text: Travel arrangements and accommodations will be provided by the COMPANY. This will be standard accommodations regardless of availability of first-class options.
perturbation.explanation: The modification downgrades the accommodation standard, potentially conflicting with implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially if the CELEBRITY's brand is tied to luxury and high-end services.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this agreement might not be strictly governed by the UCC, the principle of good faith and fair dealing is generally implied in contracts. Downgrading accommodations to 'standard' regardless of first-class availability could be interpreted as acting in bad faith if the CELEBRITY's brand and performance are associated with luxury, thus potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification in the contract text, which downgrades the accommodation standard from first class to standard regardless of availability, contradicts the implied duty of good faith, especially considering the CELEBRITY's brand. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' While the contract may not be governed by UCC, the good faith principle is implied. The change impacts the essence of the agreement, potentially failing to uphold good faith.
